examples.title: Pigeonhole Principle Check Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to verify if pigeonhole principle guarantees collision and find minimum guaranteed count
examples.code:
ALGORITHM PigeonholeCheck(n, m)
// n = number of items (pigeons), m = number of containers (holes)
BEGIN
    IF n <= m THEN
        PRINT "No guarantee of repetition"
        RETURN 0
    ELSE
        min_guaranteed = 1
        // Calculate using ceiling formula
        min_guaranteed = (n + m - 1) / m  // Integer division = ceil(n/m)
        PRINT "At least one container has ", min_guaranteed, " items"
        RETURN min_guaranteed
    END IF
END

// Example usage:
// PigeonholeCheck(101, 100) → At least one container has 2 items
// PigeonholeCheck(51, 12)  → At least one container has 5 items
examples.explanation: This algorithm implements the basic pigeonhole principle formula. For n items and m containers, it calculates the minimum guaranteed maximum using ceiling division.

examples.title: Birthday Paradox Probability Calculator
examples.description: Calculate probability of shared birthday using combinatorial approach
examples.code:
import math

def birthday_paradox(n_people, n_days=365):
    """
    Calculate probability that at least two people share a birthday.
    Uses the complement: P(shared) = 1 - P(all unique)
    """
    # Probability of no shared birthday
    prob_unique = 1.0
    for i in range(n_people):
        prob_unique *= (n_days - i) / n_days
    
    prob_shared = 1 - prob_unique
    return prob_shared

# Find minimum people for >50% probability
for n in range(1, 100):
    prob = birthday_paradox(n)
    if prob > 0.5:
        print(f"Minimum people for >50% chance: {n}")
        print(f"Probability with {n} people: {prob:.4f}")
        break

# Calculate for different group sizes
sizes = [10, 20, 23, 30, 50, 70]
print("\nBirthday Paradox Probabilities:")
for size in sizes:
    print(f"{size} people: {birthday_paradox(size)*100:.2f}%")
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates the birthday paradox. It calculates the probability of at least two people sharing a birthday by computing the complement (all unique birthdays). The result shows 23 people exceed 50% probability, demonstrating the pigeonhole principle in probabilistic context.

examples.title: Hash Table Collision Detection using Pigeonhole Principle
examples.description: Demonstrates collision guarantee when load factor exceeds 1
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates the pigeonhole principle in hash tables. The function check_pigeonhole_guarantee shows that when items exceed slots, collision is mathematically guaranteed. This explains why hash tables maintain load factor < 1 for performance.
